{"step_by_step_thinking": "Based on the provided documents, the key information is that after the myosin head binds to actin, it undergoes a conformational change when ATP binds to it, causing it to detach from actin. This is evident from multiple sources, such as Document [0] which states 'Subsequently, there is a power stroke of the myosin head which forces the actin filament centrally towards the sarcomere, and ADP and P detach from the myosin head resulting in contraction.' and Document [1] which mentions 'Consequently, myosin dissociates from actin and the myosin head becomes cocked toward the end of the sarcomere. The ATP bound to myosin becomes hydrolyzed to ADP and inorganic phosphate (P), which both remain linked to myosin. In its cocked position, myosin then binds to a new site on the actin, creating a power stroke that pulls the actin filaments.' Therefore, the detachment of the myosin head from actin is triggered by the hydrolysis of ATP to ADP and Pi.", "answer_choice": "D"}
